channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: U.N. Blacklists Israel, Russia for Sexual Violence in Conflict
item.description: Both countries have refuted the allegations and accused the agency of bias.

item.title: What Iran Stands to Gain From a Truce Deal With the United States
item.description: U.S. concessions could include the unfreezing of assets and unsanctioning of oil.

item.title: As Trade Talks Begin, U.S.-Mexico Ties Falter
item.description: Bilateral relations have reached a breaking point at a crucial moment for the USMCA.

item.title: What Magyar Means for MAGA
item.description: Mellower European populists have killed some Republicans’ dreams of a far-right alliance.

item.title: The Dollar Was—and Remains—an Accident
item.description: As a new book shows, the dollar isn’t really the United States’ currency, but a 500-year-old relic.

item.title: Authoritarian Transformation in Istanbul’s Old City
item.description: Two new books seek to capture the changing reality of Turkey and the world.

item.title: India’s Great Political Realignment
item.description: In recent years, the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party’s focus on religious identity, together with its “purification” of voter rolls, has created a more binary—and potentially volatile—political landscape, where elections increasingly reflect ethno-religious identity. This month’s elections in five states largely confirmed this trend.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of May 23: The U.S. strikes Iran, European countries summon Russian diplomats, and the WHO calls for a cease-fire in Congo.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The new shape of war
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: Should you use a sleep tracker?
item.description: They are pretty accurate. But they could keep you up at night

item.title: Syria Wants to Replace the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The country hopes to fund its reconstruction by serving as the Middle East’s new transit and logistics hub.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The most important wars forgotten by the West
item.description: Emma Irving, a news editor, examines the enduring impact of the opium wars

item.title: Central Banking in an Age of Global Supply Shocks
item.description: The core assumptions underlying the decades-old inflation-targeting orthodoxy have proven to be unfit for a geopolitically fragmented world characterized by frequent supply-side disruptions. Under such conditions, central banks' traditional monetary policies are only as effective as markets believe them to be.

item.title: Of Course It’s a War
item.description: Trump’s legal evasion regarding Iran reveals the rotten state of constitutional war powers.

item.title: No Enemies, Stronger Army: Kazakhstan’s Strategic Bet
item.description: Kazakhstan’s military modernization efforts reflect an adaptive response to growing great power competition.

item.title: What Does Quad’s New Surveillance Initiative Mean for Indian Ocean Security?
item.description: An Indian Ocean-focused initiative is welcome. But it will need to support a democratic information-sharing culture, if it is to attract participation from all regional countries.

item.title: The Tech-MAGA Breakup Is Coming
item.description: Donald Trump has managed to fuse a backward-looking populist movement with a Silicon Valley vanguard whose guiding assumption is that AI will render a large share of ordinary people economically redundant. But with the tech billionaires now rewiring America's electricity grid and driving up costs, a split seems inevitable.

item.title: Why Does Okinawa Have So Many US Military Bases?
item.description: Author Jon Mitchell’s new book offers some answers.

item.title: What the 1980s ‘Japan Panic’ Tells Us About Today’s ‘China Threat’
item.description: It’s almost eerie how easily contemporary China discourse parallels U.S. rhetoric about Japan in the 1980s – especially given the huge differences between the two countries.

item.title: South Korea’s Matchmaking Boom Is Turning Inequality Into Compatibility
item.description: South Korea’s demographic crisis is exposing a deeper problem: how class, gender, education, housing, and family background shape who is considered marriageable.

item.title: Penpa Tsering’s Re-election and the Institutional Future of the Tibetan Movement
item.description: The Tibetan movement has long been dependent on the personal moral authority of the Dalai Lama. In a post-Dalai Lama world, the Central Tibetan Administration will need to step up.

item.title: China and Maritime Chokepoints: Hormuz, Malacca, and Indo-Pacific Vulnerability
item.description: Disruptions in Hormuz immediately generate concern regarding Malacca, another vital maritime chokepoint.

item.title: How the China-US Reset Recasts the Quad
item.description: With the shift in China-U.S. relations, India is emerging as the strategic cog in the U.S. wheel, Pakistan as a tactical partner, NATO as the anchor of the Atlantic alliance, and the Quad as a tool to serve the Indo‑Pacific.

item.title: A Professor Should Work Hard and Grade Easy
item.description: The Harvard University faculty has now decided to limit the share of “A” grades given to students to about 20%. But, as the move's defenders freely admit, the intent is to increase professors' power and students' stress, not to generate excitement in the classroom.

item.title: Strategic Implications of Myanmar’s Offensive in Chin State
item.description: The Tatmadaw’s advances will have implications not only for Myanmar’s internal conflict, but for neighboring India and Bangladesh.

item.title: What’s Behind the EU-Mexico Trade Deal?
item.description: With USMCA on ice, Mexico City is looking across the Atlantic for new partners.

item.title: Anti-War Orthodox Priest Arrested in Kazakhstan to Face Forced Psychiatric Treatment
item.description: Hieromonk Iakov Vorontsov, arrested on drug-related charges after attempting to establish a non-Moscow aligned Orthodox church in Kazakhstan, now faces forced psychological treatment reminiscent of Soviet-era repression.

item.title: What Price Hormuz?
item.description: Donald Trump has three primary objectives in the Persian Gulf: unwind the Iranian nuclear program, permanently reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and not be the president blamed for having “lost” Taiwan to China. The bad news for the US president is that he can achieve at most two of these goals.

item.title: The Mismeasurement of Europe’s Productivity
item.description: The Nobel laureate economist Paul Krugman uses the wrong metric—purchasing power parity—to make the case that Europe is not lagging far behind the US in terms of productivity. The right metric shows that the gap is growing.

item.title: Are China and Pakistan Rebranding CPEC Through B2B Conferences?
item.description: Unlike Chinese investments in the past, which were state-backed and focused on infrastructure, the B2B model is more commercial and a business-driven partnership.

item.title: Indonesia’s Aceh Province Continues Caning as a Form of Punishment
item.description: But the ultra-conservative Indonesian province is not the only place in Southeast Asia where the punishment is used.

item.title: Why Southeast Asia’s Just Energy Transition Partnerships Have Stalled
item.description: Despite high hopes and billions in funding pledges, the scheme has paid insufficient attention to the political and economic realities of the recipient countries.

item.title: Don’t Count the Quad Out
item.description: A more modest agenda is also more credible.

item.title: Don’t Count the Quad Out
item.description: A more modest agenda is also more credible.

item.title: China’s AI Heist
item.description: How to counter Beijing’s unauthorized "distillation."

item.title: Cuba’s Only Option
item.description: A deal with Washington is the island’s best hope.

item.title: Cuba’s Only Choice
item.description: A deal with Washington is the island’s best hope.

item.title: The United States’ Rare Earth Strategy Faces a Durability Test
item.description: Rare earth projects require a solid commitment across political cycles, a challenge that the United States has not yet been able to overcome.

item.title: Bowing to online fury, China’s censors ban a prizewinning film
item.description: Both nationalists and the manosphere resented its portrayal of their country
